followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: After reading the other reviewers, I keep the same broad verdict but lower the overall assessment slightly. The positive checks on Appendix B increase my confidence in the local Minkowski construction, while the other reviewers' Duff/dimensionless-constants and Galilei-necessity objections reinforce that the paper's 'set to rest' framing is too strong.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: The claim that the main one-standard result is simply 'well supported' within the paper's operational definition understates the unproved scope of 'all observables' and the imported dynamical assumptions.

The objection that the mole/Avogadro number dismissal is a technical flaw is weak for this paper's purpose; treating amount of substance as a count is standard and does not materially affect the central unit-count argument.

The suggestion that quantum-gravitational appearances of G by themselves refute the paper is too strong; they mainly show that the paper's regime should be stated as classical/semiclassical and not a quantum-gravity resolution.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The Appendix B derivation of the three-clock protocol is algebraically sound in Minkowski spacetime and is a genuine strength of the paper.
The paper does not fully answer Duff's point that dimensionless constants, not dimensional unit choices, carry convention-independent physical content.
The equivalence principle and constancy of G and other conversion constants are real assumptions, not optional presentation details.
The Galilei-spacetime claim that rulers are strictly necessary needs more than the failure of the specific Unruh protocol.
The curved-spacetime version needs local hypotheses; tidal effects and geodesic-deviation issues are a valid caution.

main_claims.claim: The DOV controversy can be reformulated as the minimum number of apparatuses or standards needed for distinct laboratories to compare observable values.
main_claims.notes: This is a coherent operational definition for dimensional standards. It is not forced by all sides of DOV, especially Duff's view that only dimensionless constants have invariant physical significance.

main_claims.claim: Galilei spacetime requires bona fide clocks and rulers, whereas Minkowski/relativistic spacetime requires bona fide clocks only.
main_claims.notes: The contrast is plausible and well motivated. The clock-only construction is explicitly shown in Minkowski spacetime; the strict necessity of rulers in Galilei spacetime and the full curved-spacetime extension are less fully proved.
main_claims.paper_location: Section 'Galilei and Minkowski spacetimes'; Section 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes'

main_claims.claim: In Galilei spacetime, all observables can be expressed using only space and time units by absorbing mass into G.
main_claims.notes: The algebra works once Newtonian gravity, universal G, and equivalence of inertial and gravitational mass are assumed. This is not a consequence of Galilei spacetime alone.
main_claims.paper_location: Section 'Two units to rule us all in Galilei spacetime', Eqs. (13)-(15)

main_claims.claim: In relativistic spacetimes, all observables can be expressed using only one time unit; hence the number of fundamental constants is one.
main_claims.notes: The length-to-time part is well supported in the idealized Minkowski setting. The extension to all non-spacetime observables depends on treating G, c, k_e, and k_B as universal conversion factors for the intended physical domain.
main_claims.paper_location: Sections 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes' and 'One fundamental constant', Eqs. (18)-(22)

main_claims.claim: Equation (18) gives a speed-independent clock-only determination of a rod's proper length in Minkowski spacetime.
main_claims.notes: Appendix B's derivation is sound in the stated special-relativistic setup with an inertial rod and freely moving clocks. The reviewer claims that this algebra checks out are valid.
main_claims.paper_location: Section 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes' and Appendix B

missing_assumptions.assumption: The intended domain is classical or semiclassical physics with a fixed set of known observables, not literally every theory definable over Galilei or relativistic spacetime.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Introduction and repeated claims in Sections 'Two units to rule us all...' and 'Time to rule us all...'
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The proof is a dimensional re-expression for known physical quantities; unrestricted new sectors could require additional normalization conventions or dimensionful parameters.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Newtonian gravity with universal, constant G and equivalence of inertial and gravitational mass is available for the Galilei-spacetime reduction.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section 'Two units to rule us all in Galilei spacetime'
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This is the only mechanism given for eliminating the mass standard. If it fails, the two-standard conclusion changes.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The constants k_B, k_e, G, and c are universal and stable conversion factors over the measurement domain.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sections 'Recuperating the MKS system from the SI' and 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes'
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If any vary spatially, temporally, or by sector, they carry empirical information rather than serving as globally shareable unit conversions.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Ideal or sufficiently good bona fide clocks exist in the regime where the spacetime concept is being used.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section 'Galilei and Minkowski spacetimes'; Closing remarks
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The authors acknowledge quantum limits to clocks, but the main operational construction assumes clocks satisfying ideal proper-time behavior.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the foundations/metrology literature already contain essentially the same spacetime-first answer to DOV, including the reduction from two standards to one in relativistic spacetime?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Assessing novelty requires a targeted literature comparison beyond the provided bundle.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the Unruh three-clock protocol original, correctly attributed, and sufficient for the stated local curved-spacetime generalization?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The Minkowski algebra can be checked from the text, but provenance and curved-spacetime scope require expert literature and differential-geometric spot-checking.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Can one prove within the Galilei-spacetime axioms that no clock-only operational protocol can recover spatial distance?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper only shows one protocol fails; a no-go theorem over all possible protocols needs a human mathematical check.

novelty_assessment: The synthesis is useful and the spacetime-first organization is cleaner than many discussions of the DOV issue. The Unruh clock protocol, at least as presented here, is an elegant technical highlight. However, much of the conversion-factor reasoning for c, G, k_B, and electromagnetic units is standard geometrized-units/metrology material, and the paper does not fully settle the deeper dimensionless-constants debate. I therefore view the novelty as moderate rather than field-shaping.
score_rationale: The paper is clear and conceptually worthwhile, and the key Minkowski clock protocol is technically solid. I lower the score from my prior review because the other reviews made the unresolved Duff/dimensionless-constants issue and the lack of a Galilei no-go argument more salient. The work remains promising as a precise operational reframing, but its strongest universal and resolution claims need sharper hypotheses.
summary: The paper gives a spacetime-first operational answer to the Duff-Okun-Veneziano unit-counting question: Galilei spacetime needs clocks and rulers, while relativistic spacetime needs clocks only, so all dimensional observables can be expressed using one time standard. The local Minkowski clock protocol for length is a strong and useful component. I still do not regard the paper as settling the whole DOV controversy, because the global 'all observables' conclusion imports dynamical assumptions about gravity, charge, temperature, and constant conversion factors, and it largely brackets Duff's dimensionless-constants point.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper eliminates kg by replacing each MKS observable with G^{gamma_i} times that observable. This requires mass to be operationally calibrated through GM=aL^2 and requires inertial and gravitational mass to coincide. The paper notes the equivalence-principle issue, but the headline claim is broader than this conditional argument.
technical_objections.objection: The reduction of mass to length and time in Galilei spacetime imports Newtonian gravity, universal constant G, and equivalence of inertial and gravitational mass rather than following from Galilei spacetime alone.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section 'Two units to rule us all in Galilei spacetime', paragraph beginning 'Maxwell’s statement emphasizes...' and Eqs. (13)-(15)

technical_objections.evidence: The formal argument treats observables as monomials in chosen dimensional units and conversion constants. It does not precisely delimit dimensionless couplings, theory-dependent field normalizations, particle labels, topological observables, or observables in sectors where the relevant conversion law is absent or varying.
technical_objections.objection: The universal quantifier over 'all observables' remains underdefined.
technical_objections.paper_location: Introduction; Section 'Recuperating the MKS system from the SI'; Eqs. (14)-(15), (19)-(22)

technical_objections.evidence: Eq. (18) supports expressing length in time units. But eliminating mass, charge, and temperature dimensions uses G, k_e, and k_B as stable universal conversion factors. This is a statement about the physical laws under consideration, not a consequence of relativistic spacetime structure itself.
technical_objections.objection: The move from clock-only measurement of spacetime lengths to clock-only expression of all physical observables in relativistic theories requires extra dynamical and empirical hypotheses.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes', transition from Eq. (18) to Eqs. (19)-(20); Section 'One fundamental constant'

technical_objections.evidence: The paper replies that e^S and hbar^S can be compared with bona fide clocks. That establishes comparability after choosing a dimensional standard, but it does not show that such dimensional comparisons have convention-independent physical content equivalent to dimensionless constants such as the fine-structure constant or mass ratios.
technical_objections.objection: The paper's claim to resolve the DOV controversy is overstated because Duff's dimensionless-constant objection is not fully answered.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract; Introduction; Section 'One fundamental constant'; Closing remarks

technical_objections.evidence: The derivations use inertial congruences, a rod at rest, freely moving clocks, and Minkowski geometry. In curved spacetime the construction is only local and requires convex-normal-neighborhood, tidal-correction, simultaneity, and clock-transport qualifications. The other reviewers' tidal-force concern is a valid version of this point, though minor if the claim is explicitly local.
technical_objections.objection: The general-relativistic extension is asserted more broadly than derived.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section 'Minkowski spacetime' after Eq. (4); Section 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes' after Eq. (18); Appendix B final paragraph

technical_objections.evidence: The fact that Eq. (18) yields D=0 when tau_3=tau_1+tau_2 shows that this specific relativistic clock protocol cannot define Galilean length. It does not by itself rule out every possible clock-only protocol under the Galilei axioms.
technical_objections.objection: The strict necessity of bona fide rulers in Galilei spacetime is not proved by showing only that the Unruh protocol degenerates.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section 'Time to rule us all in relativistic spacetimes', paragraph beginning 'In fact, it is easy to verify...'
